options.why: Found the numeric HCF but dropped $x$, which is common to both terms.
Kept the variable but used $1$ instead of the HCF of $6$ and $9$, which is $3$.
Used the lowest common multiple of $6$ and $9$ instead of their highest common factor.
Included $x$, but $x$ is not a factor of $15$, so it is not common to both terms.
Missed that $5$ divides both $10$ and $15$; treated the terms as sharing no factor.
Took the first coefficient as the HCF, but $10$ does not divide $15$.
Took the higher power $x^5$; the HCF uses the lower power $x^2$.
Added the exponents $2+5$; shared powers are compared, not added.
Used $2$, a common factor of $8$ and $12$, instead of their highest common factor $4$.
Kept $y$, but $y$ appears only in the first term, so it is not common.
Found the numeric HCF but dropped $x$, which is common to both terms.
Used $2$, a common factor of $6$ and $12$, rather than their highest common factor $6$.

questions.solution_text:
$\text{HCF of } 6, 12 = 6$
$x \text{ in both, } y \text{ is not}$
$= 6x$

options.why: The HCF of $7$ and $13$ is $1$, but the shared variable $a$ must still be included.
Multiplied $7$ and $13$; that gives a common multiple, not the highest common factor.
Assumed the smaller coefficient is the HCF, but $7$ does not divide $13$.
Took the higher power of each variable and kept $c$; the HCF uses the lower power and only shared variables.
Included $c$, but $c$ appears only in the first term, so it is not common.
Used $2$, a common factor of $18$ and $24$, instead of their highest common factor $6$.

questions.solution_text:
$\text{HCF of } 18, 24 = 6$
$a^2 \text{ and } b^2 \text{ are lower powers}$
$c \text{ is not in both}$
$= 6a^2b^2$
